1: Chapter 13 introduces the first of Jeremiah's great sign-acts — symbolic actions the prophet performs at YHWH's command that embody the message in physical form. Thus YHWH said to me: Go and buy a linen loincloth and put it around your waist, and do not dip it in water. The linen belt (ezor pishtim) is a garment of intimate contact — worn against the skin, symbolizing intimate relationship. Linen was the priestly garment material (Leviticus 6:10; Jeremiah 16:4), connecting the image to sacred function. The prohibition against washing it is deliberate: this belt has never been ritually cleansed.
2: So I bought a loincloth according to the word of YHWH, and put it around my waist. Jeremiah's obedience is immediate and unquestioned — a pattern throughout the book's sign-act narratives (cf. Jeremiah 19, 27–28, 32). The prophet performs the commanded action before understanding its interpretation, modeling the covenant response of hearing and doing. The physical action makes the message visible, embodied, and memorable in a predominantly oral culture.
3: And the word of YHWH came to me a second time, saying — the two-stage structure of the sign-act (do this → now do that) mirrors the narrative theology of the act. The first command establishes the object; the second command initiates the process of its ruin. The two-stage form is itself instructive: YHWH did not begin with destruction but with intimacy. The ruin of the relationship is a second movement, not the first.
4: Take the loincloth that you have bought, which is around your waist, and arise, go to the Euphrates and hide it there in a cleft of the rock. The Euphrates (Perat) is Babylon's river — the seat of the coming exile and the location of the nation that will destroy Judah. The act of hiding the belt at the Euphrates is a prophetic anticipation of what will happen to Judah: it will be carried off to Babylonian territory, hidden from view, left to deteriorate.
5: So I went and hid it by the Euphrates, as YHWH commanded me. Some commentators argue that the Euphrates reference is literal (requiring two long journeys of ~700 miles each) while others suggest it refers to Parah, a village near Anathoth (Joshua 18:23), a much shorter journey. The symbolic weight of the Euphrates as the Babylonian exile destination likely drives the text regardless of the physical geography. What matters theologically is the direction of the concealment: toward Babylon, away from home.
6: And after many days YHWH said to me, Arise, go to the Euphrates, and take from there the loincloth that I commanded you to hide there. After many days — the time gap is significant. The deterioration is not instantaneous; it takes time. The exile will not happen overnight but through a process of increasingly severe judgments. The return to retrieve the ruined belt dramatizes the moment of exposure: what has been happening in secret will be revealed.
7: Then I went to the Euphrates, and dug, and I took the loincloth from the place where I had hidden it. And behold, the loincloth was spoiled; it was good for nothing. The belt retrieved from the earth and moisture is rotted, useless, destroyed. Nishchat (spoiled, ruined, corrupted) — the same verb used in Jeremiah 18 for the clay vessel spoiled in the potter's hands. What was beautiful and functional is now valueless. This is the condition to which Judah's pride has led.
8: Then the word of YHWH came to me — the interpretive oracle follows the sign-act. This two-part structure (act then word) is characteristic of prophetic sign-acts throughout the Hebrew Bible. The act creates a question in the audience; the word answers it. The method is pedagogically powerful: the physical image remains in memory even when the verbal explanation fades.
9: Thus says YHWH: Even so will I spoil the pride of Judah and the great pride of Jerusalem. The ga'on (pride) that will be spoiled is not merely arrogance but the cultural, religious, and national self-confidence of Judah — the belief that YHWH's dwelling in Jerusalem made the city inviolable, that Temple sacrifice secured divine favor, that being Abraham's descendants guaranteed protection. This is the pride that Jeremiah has been attacking throughout the book. The loincloth's ruin is the pride's ruin.
10: This evil people, who refuse to hear my words, who stubbornly follow their own heart and have gone after other gods to serve them and worship them, shall be like this loincloth, which is good for nothing. The interpretation is explicit: the rotted belt equals the people who have abandoned the covenant. Stubbornly follow their own heart (sherirut libam) is the diagnostic phrase from Jeremiah 11:8. The people have chosen autonomy over covenant; the result is the same as the unburied belt — ruin in a foreign land.
11: For as the loincloth clings to the waist of a man, so I made the whole house of Israel and the whole house of Judah cling to me, declares YHWH, that they might be for me a people, a name, a praise, and a glory, but they would not listen. The positive purpose of the covenant relationship is stated with striking intimacy: Israel was meant to cling (davaq) to YHWH as a garment clings to the body — inseparable, intimate, bearing the shape of the one to whom it is attached. The covenant design was that Israel would be YHWH's glory before the nations. The tragedy is total: the design was perfect; the failure was the people's refusal to listen.
12: You shall speak to them this word: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel, 'Every jar is filled with wine'. A second sign — now involving ceramic jars and wine. The proverb every jar is filled with wine was apparently a familiar saying. The audience would likely respond with the obvious: yes, of course jars are made to hold wine. Jeremiah inverts their assumption: he is not speaking of literal wine-filling but of judgment filling.
13: And they will say to you, 'Do we not indeed know that every jar is filled with wine?' Then you shall say to them, 'Thus says YHWH: Behold, I will fill with drunkenness all the inhabitants of this land: the kings who sit on David's throne, the priests, the prophets, and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem'. The wine is not blessing but intoxication leading to staggering and falling — a classic biblical image of divine judgment (cf. Psalms 60:3; Isaiah 51:17–23; Jeremiah 25:15–29). Every level of Judahite society is included: kings, priests, prophets, ordinary inhabitants. The judgment is universal within the covenant community.
14: And I will dash them one against another, fathers and sons together, declares YHWH. I will not pity or spare or have compassion, that I should not destroy them. The violence of the image — family members dashed against one another — pictures the social collapse of invasion and siege. Civil order dissolves; families turn on each other. The triple negation of divine compassion (I will not pity, I will not spare, I will not have compassion) intensifies the finality: this is not a warning but an announcement. The time for compassion has passed.
15: Hear and give ear; be not proud, for YHWH has spoken. A direct appeal interrupts the judgment oracles. The exhortation to hear (shim'u veshe'ezinu) against pride (ga'avah) links back to the loincloth sign: the pride that will be ruined is first of all a refusal to listen. Hearing and humility are synonymous here; pride is the deafness that prevents the word of YHWH from penetrating the heart.
16: Give glory to YHWH your God before he brings darkness, before your feet stumble on the twilight mountains, and while you look for light he turns it into gloom and makes it deep darkness. Give glory is the call to honor YHWH rather than idols — the same competitive dynamic throughout the polemic of Jeremiah 10–13. The twilight mountains (harei neshef) image the moment before night fully falls — the last moment of possible return. The coming darkness is the Babylonian invasion and exile. There is still a window of repentance, but it is closing.
17: But if you will not listen, my soul will weep in secret for your pride; my eyes will weep bitterly and run down with tears, because YHWH's flock has been taken captive. The prophet weeps in anticipation of the exile he cannot prevent. The weeping in secret (bimasterin tivkeh nafshi) suggests Jeremiah's tears are not performative grief for public effect but genuine anguish hidden from view. YHWH's flock taken captive: the shepherd-flock imagery from 10:21 returns — the scattered sheep are the exiled people, and the prophet weeps for them.
18: Say to the king and the queen mother: 'Take a lowly seat, for your beautiful crown has come down from your head'. The king addressed is likely Jehoiachin, who was taken to Babylon in 597 BC after a brief three-month reign (2 Kings 24:8–17); the queen mother is Nehushta. The removal of the crown dramatizes the end of Davidic sovereignty — not permanently (the Davidic covenant remains in Jeremiah 33:14–26) but in immediate historical terms. Humility (shafal) is the only posture available to those stripped of power.
19: The cities of the Negeb are shut up, with none to open them; all Judah is taken into exile, wholly taken into exile. The Negeb is the southern desert territory of Judah — the last geographic buffer before complete territorial loss. Once the Negeb cities are sealed (by siege or conquest), there is no exit. The repetition wholly taken into exile (galat shalom giltat) uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis — the totality of the deportation is stressed. No one escapes; every stratum of society is displaced.
20: Lift up your eyes and see those who come from the north. Where is the flock that was given you, your beautiful flock? — Jerusalem is addressed (feminine). The north enemy is the Babylonian army, visible on the horizon. The question about the beautiful flock is both geographical (where are your people?) and covenantal (where is the community entrusted to your shepherding?). The leaders of Jerusalem received a people to tend; they have lost them through unfaithfulness.
21: What will you say when they set as head over you those whom you yourself have taught to be friends to you? Will not pangs take hold of you like those of a woman in labor? — the allies Judah cultivated through diplomacy and treaty — Egypt, Assyria at various points — will be the instruments of her destruction. The ones taught as friends become masters. The labor pangs image for unexpected, overwhelming distress is recurring throughout the prophets (Isaiah 13:8; Jeremiah 26:17; Micah 4:9–10; 1 Thessalonians 5:3).
22: And if you say in your heart, 'Why have these things come upon me?' it is for the greatness of your iniquity that your skirts are lifted up and you suffer violence. The question why has this come upon me? is the wrong question — a question of the willfully ignorant. The answer is direct: covenantal iniquity. The imagery of skirts lifted up is the language of violent exposure and sexual humiliation — frequently used in prophetic literature to describe military conquest and the public shame of a captured city-woman (cf. Nahum 3:5; Ezekiel 16:37).
23: Can the Ethiopian change his skin or the leopard his spots? Then also you can do good who are accustomed to do evil. One of Scripture's most powerful statements on the depth of moral corruption. The rhetorical questions expect the answer no: skin and spots are unchangeable natural facts. The prophet is not denying the possibility of divine transformation (cf. Jeremiah 31:33 — I will put my law within them; Ezekiel 36:26 — a new heart) but the possibility of self-generated moral reformation. Human beings accustomed to evil (limudei hara') cannot reform themselves from within. This anthropological realism drives the necessity of the New Covenant.
24: I will scatter you like chaff driven by the wind from the desert. The wind-scattering of chaff is a classic biblical image for judgment (Psalms 1:4; Isaiah 17:13; Jeremiah 29:5; Jeremiah 41:16) — the lightweight, worthless remains of threshing carried off in all directions by the threshing-floor wind. Israel's scattering in exile is not random but purposive: the judgment of the covenant God who is also the lord of wind and weather.
25: This is your lot, the portion I have measured out to you, declares YHWH, because you have forgotten me and trusted in lies. The exile is described as a goral (lot, portion) — the same word used for land-allotments in Joshua, now applied to judgment. What was given as blessing (land as inheritance) becomes the template for what is taken away as curse. The theological root of the disaster is stated simply: you have forgotten me (vatishkechi oti) and trusted in lies (vativtechi beshaqer) — the two defections that summarize the entire book's indictment.
26: I myself will lift up your skirts over your face, and your shame will be seen. The divine action of public exposure (see v22) is now attributed directly to YHWH — not merely permitted but enacted. The shame of conquest is absorbed into the covenant-judgment framework: YHWH is not a passive observer of Babylon's violence against Jerusalem but the active agent behind it. This theological claim — that YHWH uses pagan nations as instruments of his judgment — is the most difficult and central claim of the entire prophetic tradition.
27: I have seen your abominations, your adulteries and neighings, your lewd whorings, on the hills in the field. Woe to you, O Jerusalem! How long will it be before you are made clean? The chapter closes with a woe-lament (oi lakh, woe to you) — the form of mourning over those already dead or doomed. The accumulated sexual imagery (adultery, neighing, whorings) depicts Israel's covenant infidelity as comprehensive, habitual, and public. The closing question — how long will it be before you are made clean? — leaves the chapter on a note of suspended anguish, neither promise nor condemnation but the grief of a God who has been betrayed by the people he chose and cannot simply unmake his love for them.
